content:
# Genesis Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9r–9v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_016_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_016_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0015

---

## The God Who Sees

**1** Now Sarai, Abram's wife, had not been able to give him children. She had an Egyptian slave-girl named Hagar, **2** and Sarai said to Abram, "Look — the LORD has kept me from having children. Go in to my slave-girl. Perhaps I can be built up through her." And Abram listened to what Sarai said.

**3** So after Abram had been living in Canaan for ten years, Sarai his wife took her Egyptian slave-girl Hagar and gave her to her husband Abram as a wife. **4** He slept with Hagar, and she became pregnant. And when she realized she was pregnant, she began to look on her mistress with contempt.

**5** Then Sarai said to Abram, "This wrong is on you. I put my slave-girl in your arms, and now that she knows she's pregnant, she looks at me with contempt. May the LORD judge between you and me." **6** Abram said to Sarai, "Your slave-girl is in your hands. Do with her whatever you think is right." And Sarai treated her so harshly that Hagar ran away from her.

**7** The angel of the LORD found her by a spring of water in the wilderness — the spring on the road to Shur. **8** He said, "Hagar, slave-girl of Sarai — where have you come from, and where are you going?"

She said, "I'm running away from my mistress Sarai."

**9** The angel of the LORD said to her, "Go back to your mistress and submit to her authority." **10** The angel of the LORD also said to her, "I will make your descendants so many they cannot be counted."

**11** The angel of the LORD said to her:

> "You are pregnant, and you will give birth to a son.
> You shall name him Ishmael —
> *God hears* —
> because the LORD has heard your affliction.
> **12** He will be a wild man, free as a wild donkey,
> his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him,
> and he will live at odds with all his kin."

**13** Then she called the name of the LORD who had spoken to her: "You are El Roi" — for she said, "Have I really seen God and lived to tell it?" **14** That is why the well was called Beer-lahai-roi — the Well of the Living One Who Sees Me. It lies between Kadesh and Bered.

**15** Hagar gave birth to Abram's son, and Abram named the son that Hagar bore him Ishmael. **16** Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to him.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 16 — the God who finds you

If Genesis 15 is about a man who argues with God under the stars and is called righteous for it, Genesis 16 is about a woman who has no voice in the decisions that shape her life — and discovers that God has been watching all along. It is a chapter about power and powerlessness, about the way human schemes produce human suffering, and about a God who breaks every convention by showing up not to the patriarch but to the slave.

Hagar's story is startling in its specificity. She is Egyptian, not Israelite. She is a woman in a world where women's choices were severely circumscribed. She is a slave — property in the legal sense. She has no narrative agency: Sarai forms the plan, Abram agrees to it, Hagar is "given" to him as one hands over an object. She does not speak until she is spoken to. And yet she is the one who receives the first annunciation in the Bible, the first appearance of the angel of the LORD in the Bible, and the only moment in all of Scripture when a human being names God.

That is not an accident of the text. It is the text's entire point.

---

### Verses 1–3 — Sarai's plan: the logic of the desperate

**וְשָׂרַי אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם לֹא יָלְדָה לוֹ** — "Sarai, Abram's wife, had not borne him children." The chapter opens with the sentence that has been hanging over everything since chapter 12. The promise, the calling, the covenant of chapter 15 — all of it pivots on the fact that they still have no child. The absence is stated flatly, without explanation, without comfort. It is simply the condition of their lives.

**וְלָהּ שִׁפְחָה מִצְרִית וּשְׁמָהּ הָגָר** — "And she had an Egyptian slave-girl whose name was Hagar." The second character is introduced not as a person but as a possession — defined first by her status (*shiphchah*, slave-girl), then by her ethnicity, then by her name. Hagar. The name may mean "stranger" or "fugitive" in Arabic cognates, though its Hebrew etymology is disputed. She is named before she acts, and she is named as someone else's.

Sarai's proposal in verse 2 is not impetuous or reckless. It follows an entirely established legal convention of the ancient Near East. In Mesopotamian law (attested in the Nuzi tablets and the Code of Hammurabi), if a free woman could not bear children, she could provide her slave-girl as a surrogate, with the resulting child legally attributed to the free woman. **אוּלַי אִבָּנֶה מִמֶּנָּה** — "perhaps I can be *ibaneh* through her." The verb *ibaneh* is the Niphal of *banah* — "to be built" — and it contains the word *ben* ("son") folded within it: *to be built up by a son*, *to be son-ed through her*, *to gain a family through her*. The wordplay is untranslatable in English but Sarai's desperation and cleverness live in it: she is trying to be *built up* — to construct for herself a future through this other woman's body. The translation "built up" preserves the construction metaphor even if it loses the son-pun.

**וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָם לְקוֹל שָׂרָי** — "And Abram listened to the voice of Sarai." The Hebrew is exact and its echo is deliberate. In Genesis 3:17 God said to Adam: "Because you listened to the voice (*qol*) of your wife..." The same construction. The same framing. The narratives are meant to rhyme: a man listening to a woman's voice, taking the offered solution, setting in motion consequences no one fully anticipated. Genesis is doing what it always does — folding earlier patterns into later situations, letting the alert reader hear the resonances.

---

### Verse 4 — conception and contempt

**וַתַּרְא כִּי הָרָתָה וַתֵּקַל גְּבִרְתָּהּ בְּעֵינֶיהָ** — "And she saw that she had conceived, and her mistress became light in her eyes."

The Hebrew idiom **קָלַל בְּעֵינַיִם** — "to be light in the eyes of someone" — means to be regarded with contempt, to be diminished in someone's estimation. Hagar, who had been powerless, suddenly possesses something Sarai does not: a child growing in her body. The power dynamic shifts. Or at least, Hagar perceives it as shifted. She is carrying the heir.

What she does with this is not narrated — the text does not say she speaks harshly, or acts cruelly, or openly challenges Sarai. It only says she looked at her differently. But that look is enough. In the world of the tent, where proximity is inescapable and status is everything, a changed look can be a declaration of war.

The LXX Vaticanus renders this **ἠτιμάσθη ἡ κυρία ἐναντίον αὐτῆς** — "the mistress was dishonored before her." The Greek *atimadzein* — dishonoring, treating as without value — makes explicit what the Hebrew leaves in the register of gaze. Both versions converge on the same social catastrophe: a slave has looked at her owner and found her wanting.

---

### Verse 5 — Sarai's accusation: the injury of the architect

**וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרַי אֶל-אַבְרָם חֲמָסִי עָלֶיךָ** — "And Sarai said to Abram: *my wrong is upon you*."

This is one of the most psychologically dense lines in the chapter. Sarai designed this situation. She chose Hagar. She gave her to Abram. The plan was hers. And now that the plan has produced suffering she did not anticipate, she turns to Abram and declares: **חָמָס** — *chamas* — which means violence, injustice, outrage, wrongful injury. The word is used for serious moral offenses throughout the Hebrew Bible. Sarai is not making a legal quibble. She is making a moral accusation.

The irony is not lost on the text. She put her slave-girl in Abram's arms — **נְתַתִּיהָ בְּחֵיקֶךָ** ("I gave her into your chest / into your embrace") — and now the result of that gift has turned against her. Her complaint is real even if her reasoning is circular. The injury she names — **חֲמָסִי** — is genuinely felt. The logic of her accusation, however, is self-defeating: she holds Abram responsible for the outcome of her own scheme.

**יִשְׁפֹּט יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶיךָ** — "May the LORD judge between me and you." The appeal to divine judgment is an escalation. When human legal process is unavailable or insufficient, you hand the case to God. Sarai is invoking YHWH as the adjudicator of an impasse neither she nor Abram can resolve. This formula will appear again in the Bible — most famously when Jephthah appeals to YHWH before battle, and when the accused party cannot be vindicated otherwise.

---

### Verse 6 — Abram's abdication

**וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָם אֶל-שָׂרַי הִנֵּה שִׁפְחָתֵךְ בְּיָדֵךְ** — "Abram said to Sarai: 'Here — your slave-girl is in your hand.'"

Abram abdicates. He removes himself from the situation entirely, returning Hagar to Sarai's authority with a single sentence. It is a legal act: a slave-wife who has conceived can still be treated as a slave by the first wife in certain ancient Near Eastern codes. Abram is not protecting Hagar. He is protecting the peace of his household. He is also, perhaps, protecting himself from Sarai's continuing accusation. The path of least resistance is to say: she is yours.

**וַתְּעַנֶּהָ שָׂרָי** — "And Sarai afflicted her." The verb *anah* in the Piel — to afflict, to oppress, to treat harshly. This is the same verb the Torah will use for Pharaoh's treatment of the Israelites in Egypt. **וַיְעַנּוּ אֹתָנוּ הַמִּצְרִים** — "the Egyptians afflicted us" (Deuteronomy 26:6). Sarai's treatment of Hagar prefigures — even inverts — the story of the Exodus. The Egyptian will one day oppress Israel; here an Israelite (or proto-Israelite) oppresses an Egyptian. The text does not look away from this.

**וַתִּבְרַח מִפָּנֶיהָ** — "And she fled from her face." From her presence. Hagar runs. She is pregnant, enslaved, without resources, and she runs into the wilderness. The direction she is fleeing — toward Shur, the road to Egypt — tells us something: she is going home. Or trying to. The wilderness road to Egypt is the road back to where she came from, back toward a life that preceded Sarai and Abram and all of this.

She does not make it. Instead, something finds her.

---

### Verse 7 — the first angel: the first divine encounter in the wilderness

**וַיִּמְצָאָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** — "And the angel of the LORD *found* her."

This is the first appearance of **מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה** — the *mal'akh YHWH*, the angel of the LORD — anywhere in the Bible. It is a pivotal figure who will appear throughout the patriarchal narratives, in the call of Moses, in the encounter with Gideon, in the arrest of Balaam's donkey. Here, for the first time, this divine messenger appears.

And he appears to Hagar. Not to Abram. Not to Sarai. To the slave, alone and pregnant, by a spring of water in the wilderness of Shur.

The verb **מָצָא** — "to find" — carries weight. It is not that Hagar found a messenger; the messenger *found her*. She did not seek this encounter. She was not praying, not at a sacred site, not performing a ritual. She was running away. And the divine messenger — the presence of YHWH in commissioned form — located her precisely in her flight. The wilderness was not empty. It was already occupied by the one who knew where she was.

**עַל-הָעַיִן בַּמִּדְבָּר** — "by the spring, in the wilderness." The spring and the wilderness are set against each other: water in the wasteland, life in the place of death. This is not accidental scene-setting. Throughout the biblical narrative, the wilderness is the place where God most reliably shows up — to Hagar here, to Moses at the burning bush, to Elijah fleeing Jezebel, to Israel in the forty years of desert wandering. The wilderness strips away every human structure, every social layer, every distraction. What is left is the bare human being and whatever God chooses to do with that.

---

### Verse 8 — the question God already knows the answer to

**וַיֹּאמֶר הָגָר שִׁפְחַת שָׂרַי אֵי-מִזֶּה בָאת וְאָנָה תֵלֵכִי** — "And he said: 'Hagar, slave-girl of Sarai — where have you come from and where are you going?'"

The address is striking. The angel does not say "Hagar" and leave it at that. He says "Hagar, *slave-girl of Sarai*." He names her, and he names her status. He does not pretend that she is not a slave, does not speak to her as if the social reality does not exist. But he speaks to her directly, by name, in the full acknowledgment of her situation. She is *shiphchat Sarai* — Sarai's slave-girl — and the angel of the LORD calls her by name anyway.

The two questions — *where have you come from?* and *where are you going?* — are the basic coordinates of a human life: origin and destination, past and future. They are also questions the divine messenger obviously does not need answered for information. He is asking Hagar to speak her own story out loud. To say where she is. To name what is happening to her.

**וַתֹּאמֶר מִפְּנֵי שָׂרַי גְּבִרְתִּי אָנֹכִי בֹּרַחַת** — "And she said: 'From my mistress Sarai I am fleeing.'" Her answer is one-directional. She answers only the first question. She knows where she has come from — from Sarai's cruelty — but she has no answer to the second. She does not know where she is going. She is fleeing *from*, not running *toward*. The future is blank.

---

### Verses 9–10 — return and promise

**שׁוּבִי אֶל-גְּבִרְתֵּךְ וְהִתְעַנִּי תַּחַת יָדֶיהָ** — "Go back to your mistress and submit under her hand."

This is the hardest word in the chapter. The divine messenger tells Hagar to go back to the place of her suffering. The verb **עָנָה** reappears — the same verb used in verse 6 for what Sarai did to Hagar: *affliction*. "Submit under her hand" uses the language of oppression. The angel is not pretending the return will be comfortable. He is not promising that Sarai will be kinder. He is telling Hagar to go back into a hard situation.

This has troubled readers across centuries, and it should. Whatever the social-legal logic that makes the instruction intelligible in context, the raw emotional content of the command is: return to the place that hurt you. Commentators ancient and modern have wrestled with this. What can be said is this: the angel does not give this instruction without also giving a promise. Verse 10 immediately follows: **וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֵךְ** — "I will greatly multiply your offspring." The same formula of multiplication given to Abraham (Genesis 17:2) and to Israel (Genesis 28:3) is given here, now, to Hagar. The slave-girl receives the patriarchal blessing. Her line will be too large to count.

The promise does not cancel the hardship. It accompanies it. The angel of the LORD is not promising Hagar an easy life; he is promising her that her life matters, that what comes from her will be innumerable, that she is not a footnote in someone else's story but a beginning in her own right.

---

### Verses 11–12 — the annunciation: Ishmael

**וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה הִנָּךְ הָרָה** — "The angel of the LORD said to her: 'You are pregnant.'"

This is the first annunciation scene in the Bible. The formula — divine messenger appears, announces a pregnancy, names the child, declares the child's destiny — will recur at pivotal moments: Isaac (Genesis 17–18), Samson (Judges 13), Samuel (1 Samuel 1), and in the New Testament, John the Baptist and Jesus. Every great annunciation in the biblical tradition traces its form back to this moment, spoken to a runaway Egyptian slave by a spring in the wilderness.

The child is to be named **יִשְׁמָעֵאל** — *Ishmael* — built from the components **יִשְׁמַע** (*yishma'*, "he hears") and **אֵל** (*El*, "God"): *God hears*. The reason for the name is given immediately: **כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה אֶל-עָנְיֵךְ** — "because the LORD has heard your affliction." The name is a theological statement embedded in a person: every time someone calls this child's name, they are declaring that God listened to a slave woman in the wilderness.

**עָנְיֵךְ** — your *oni*, your affliction, your suffering, your oppression. The root *anah* appears here for the third time in the chapter (Sarai *afflicted* Hagar in v.6; the angel told her to *submit* in v.9; now God has *heard her affliction* in v.11). The verbal echo is purposeful. The oppression that Sarai inflicted, the submission the angel required — both have been witnessed. Both have been heard. The very thing done to Hagar is what God names as the thing he heard.

The prophecy of verse 12 is dense and provocative:

**וְהוּא יִהְיֶה פֶּרֶא אָדָם** — "He will be a *pere' adam*."

**פֶּרֶא** is the wild donkey of the desert — the onager, the most untameable animal of the ancient Near East, associated in biblical poetry with raw freedom, with refusal to be yoked, with life lived outside the boundaries of civilization and obligation. **אָדָם** is simply "man" or "human being." A *pere' adam* is a wild-donkey man — a man as ungovernable as the desert creature, fierce in independence, unable to be domesticated. This is not an insult. In the world of the ancient Near East, it is a description of a free man who answers to no master. Ishmael, born of a slave, will be the opposite of enslaved.

**יָדוֹ בַכֹּל וְיַד כֹּל בּוֹ** — "His hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him." This describes a man at odds with every settled social order — not a villain, but someone who exists in permanent tension with the structures others submit to. He will not be at peace with the established. He will live **עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו** — "over against / in the face of all his brothers." The LXX renders this **κατὰ πρόσωπον πάντων τῶν ἀδελφῶν αὐτοῦ κατοικήσει** — "in the face of all his brothers he will dwell." It is a prophecy of fierce, enduring independence: Ishmael's descendants will survive by being uncontrollable, confronting every attempt at domination with an answering resistance.

The Islamic tradition reads these verses as the origin of the Arab peoples. The prophecy has been seen across Jewish, Christian, and Muslim exegesis as describing the enduring character of a people who trace their lineage through Hagar and Ishmael — a people who, like their ancestor, cannot be broken.

---

### Verses 13–14 — Hagar names God: El Roi

**וַתִּקְרָא שֵׁם-יְהוָה הַדֹּבֵר אֵלֶיהָ אַתָּה אֵל רֳאִי** — "And she called the name of the LORD who spoke to her: 'You are El Roi.'"

This is one of the most extraordinary verses in the Bible.

Hagar names God. This is the only place in all of Scripture where a human being gives God a name. Not Abraham. Not Moses. Not any prophet or king or priest. A runaway Egyptian slave-girl, alone by a spring in the desert, names the one who found her.

**אֵל רֳאִי** — *El Roi*. The precise meaning is contested. *Ra'ah* means "to see" — but the form **רֳאִי** can be read as "God of seeing / God who sees" (God as the one who sees) or as "God who was seen by me" (emphasizing Hagar's experience of being seen, of seeing God). Both senses are present in the Hebrew and both are theologically significant. The God who sees — who looks with attention, who does not overlook, who notices the slave woman in the wilderness — is also the God who was seen, who allowed himself to be encountered, who was visible to the woman no one else was watching.

**כִּי אָמְרָה הֲגַם הֲלֹם רָאִיתִי אַחֲרֵי רֹאִי** — "For she said: 'Have I really seen God here and lived to tell it?'"

This is an exclamation of astonishment, not a philosophical query. The ancient world assumed that to see God directly was to die — the divine presence was too overwhelming for mortal flesh. Moses will be told he cannot see God's face and live (Exodus 33:20). Jacob will wrestle with the divine and say in amazement "I have seen God face to face and yet my life was spared" (Genesis 32:30). Here, Hagar says the same thing, before Jacob, before Moses. She has looked into the face of the divine and she is still breathing. She is still here.

The naming of the well in verse 14 — **בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי** — *Beer-lahai-roi*, "the Well of the Living One who sees me" — preserves the encounter in geography. The place where God found Hagar becomes a landmark named for the fact of divine seeing. Long after Hagar is gone, the well carries her story: this is the place where the Living One saw a slave woman fleeing into the desert and called her by name. The note that it lies between Kadesh and Bered locates this theological landmark on an actual road in an actual landscape — the divine encounter happened in real space, at a real spring, on the real road from Canaan to Egypt.

The well at Beer-lahai-roi matters beyond this chapter. In Genesis 24:62 Isaac lives near it, and in Genesis 25:11 it is where Isaac settles after Abraham's death. The place where God found Hagar becomes the place where Hagar's descendant's half-brother makes his home. The land remembers.

---

### Verses 15–16 — Ishmael's birth: Abram's son

**וַתֵּלֶד הָגָר לְאַבְרָם בֵּן** — "Hagar bore Abram a son."

After the intensity of the wilderness encounter, the narrative returns to the tent. Hagar came back, as instructed. She bore a son. Abram named him — and the name he gives is exactly the name the angel announced: Ishmael. **וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָם שֵׁם-בְּנוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה הָגָר יִשְׁמָעֵאל** — "Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Ishmael." The name given to a slave's child in the wilderness by an angel reaches the patriarch's ears and is confirmed. How? The text does not explain. Presumably Hagar told Abram what had happened. The slave came back and reported the divine encounter.

The chapter closes with a biographical note: **וְאַבְרָם בֶּן-שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים בְּלֶדֶת-הָגָר אֶת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל לְאַבְרָם** — "Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram." The age notation is calendrical but also theological: Abram has been waiting eleven years since the call of chapter 12. The promise of descendants has been a decade of waiting. This child is born out of that waiting — not the child of the covenant, as the rest of the story will clarify, but a real child, a named child, Abram's first son, and the first child in the Bible to be named by divine annunciation before birth.

---

### The architecture of the chapter: who has power and who is seen

Genesis 16 is a study in power and visibility. Every character in the chapter is either exercising power over others or discovering, to their surprise, that they are not invisible.

Sarai has legal and social power over Hagar. She forms the plan, gives the order, inflicts the punishment. Abram has the power of the husband and patriarch — he agrees to the plan, then surrenders Hagar back to Sarai's authority with a sentence. In this household economy, Hagar has no power at all. She is acted upon from beginning to end.

And then she is found.

The divine messenger's appearance to Hagar is a disruption of the entire social logic of the chapter. The person with no power is the one who receives the promise, the prophecy, the divine visitation. The woman who has been defined throughout the chapter by her relationship to others — Sarai's slave-girl, Abram's secondary wife, the mother of Abram's son — is the one who gets to name God. The one whose voice is almost entirely absent from the first six verses speaks the most theologically significant words in the entire chapter: *You are the God who sees me.*

---

### El Roi and the theology of divine seeing

The name Hagar gives God — El Roi — introduces a theme that will run through the entire biblical narrative: God as the one who *sees*.

In Genesis 29:31, "the LORD saw that Leah was unloved." In Exodus 2:25, "God saw the Israelites and God knew." In Psalm 10, the wicked say "God has forgotten, he has hidden his face, he will never see" — and the psalmist insists he does see. In the New Testament, Jesus says "your Father who sees in secret will reward you" (Matthew 6:4, 6, 18). The entire biblical theology of God as attentive, as observant, as aware of hidden suffering — all of it begins here, with a name coined by a slave woman by a spring in the desert.

El Roi is not a title invented by a theologian in a library. It is a name spoken by someone in the extremity of their situation, someone who had just discovered that the emptiness of the wilderness was not empty, that the invisibility she had lived with in Sarai's household did not extend to the one who made the sky and the spring and the road to Shur. She had been unseen by everyone who mattered — and God had been watching all along.

The well is named after the experience — *Beer-lahai-roi* — the Well of the Living One who sees me. **לַחַי** (*lachai*) — of the Living One. Not "the God who sees" in the abstract, but "the *Living* God who sees *me*." The name is in the first person. It preserves not a doctrine but an encounter: this specific person, seen by this specific God, in this specific desert.

---

### Hagar and the wilderness: a recurring pattern

The story does not end here. Hagar will appear again in Genesis 21, when she and the grown Ishmael are expelled from the household after the birth of Isaac. She will wander the wilderness of Beersheba. She will run out of water. She will set her child under a bush and walk away so she does not have to watch him die. And God will hear the boy's crying — **וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר** — and the angel of God will call to Hagar from the sky, and God will open her eyes to a well of water.

The pattern of Genesis 16 is repeated in Genesis 21: wilderness, water, the angel finding Hagar, divine provision. The God of Beer-lahai-roi is the same God of Beersheba. The Living One who sees in chapter 16 is the God who hears in chapter 21. Ishmael's name — *God hears* — is not fulfilled in chapter 16, where the emphasis is on seeing. It is fulfilled in chapter 21, when God hears the crying child in the wilderness. The name given at his annunciation comes true in the crisis of his near-death.

Hagar's double appearance in the wilderness frames the whole Ishmael narrative: she is found once before his birth, once at the edge of his death. Both times, the one who finds her is the God she named. The Living One who sees her never stops seeing.

---

### The first woman to name God: what this means

The fact that Hagar names God — and that she alone, of all the characters in Scripture, names God — is not a minor textual curiosity. It is an interpretive provocation that the text is deliberately placing before the reader.

Names in Genesis matter enormously. The naming of animals (Genesis 2), the renaming of Abram to Abraham (Genesis 17), of Sarai to Sarah, of Jacob to Israel — each naming is an exercise of insight, of authority, of identification. To name something is to perceive its true nature clearly enough to speak it accurately. When God renames people, it is because God sees who they truly are beneath their circumstances. When Hagar names God, she is doing the same thing: she is seeing clearly enough, in the moment of the divine encounter, to speak the truth of who God is.

What she names him is not a title of power or majesty. She does not call him the Almighty, the Creator, the Lord of Hosts. She calls him the God who sees. She names him from the experience of being seen when she thought she was invisible. The divine attribute she identifies is attentiveness — the specific quality that mattered in her specific situation. She was unseen by the people around her. God saw her. She names him for that.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 9r–9v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 16 appears in the right-hand and central columns of folio 9r (page 001, overlapping with the closing verses of Genesis 15) and continues through folio 9v (page 002), which contains the annunciation to Hagar, the naming of El Roi, the birth of Ishmael, and the opening verses of Genesis 17. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 11–12 (pa_0011, pa_0012); the chapter marker CAP. XVIII appears at the top of pa_0011, indicating Genesis 18 in the Vaticanus numbering system; Genesis 16 text is visible in the central and right columns of pa_0011 and continuing through pa_0012, with CAP. XIX appearing in the right margin of pa_0012 marking the transition into Genesis 17.*
